news_summary: Today's space news highlights breakthroughs in understanding unique cosmic phenomena, advancements in powerful spaceflight computing technology, and inspiring contributions to lunar exploration.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées décisives dans la compréhension de phénomènes cosmiques uniques, des progrès dans le domaine des technologies informatiques de pointe pour les vols spatiaux, ainsi que des contributions inspirantes à l'exploration lunaire.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan avances en la comprensión de fenómenos cósmicos únicos, progresos en la potente tecnología informática aplicada a los vuelos espaciales y contribuciones inspiradoras a la exploración lunar.

articles.title: Interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS contains strange water never seen in our solar system
articles.summary: A mysterious comet from beyond our solar system is giving astronomers a rare glimpse into alien worlds — and it may have formed in a place far colder and stranger than anything around our Sun. The interstellar visitor, called 3I/ATLAS, contains an astonishingly high amount of “heavy water,” far exceeding anything seen in our own solar system.
articles.content:
Less than a year ago, astronomers spotted a comet passing through our solar system that originated far beyond it. The object, known as 3I/ATLAS, is only the third confirmed interstellar visitor ever detected, and scientists are now uncovering clues about the alien environment where it formed.

A new study led by researchers at the University of Michigan suggests the comet was born in conditions far colder than those that shaped our own solar system. The findings come from an analysis of the comet's unusual water composition, which revealed extraordinarily high levels of deuterium, a heavier form of hydrogen.

The research was published in the journal Nature Astronomy and received support from NASA, the U.S. National Science Foundation and Chile's National Research and Development Agency.

"Our new observations show that the conditions that led to the formation of our solar system are much different from how planetary systems evolved in different parts of our galaxy," said Luis Salazar Manzano, lead author of the study and a doctoral student in the U-M Department of Astronomy.

Alien Comet Contains Unusual "Heavy Water"

Water molecules are made up of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom, giving water its familiar H 2 O formula. In ordinary water, hydrogen atoms contain only a proton. But some forms of water contain deuterium, an isotope of hydrogen that includes both a proton and a neutron.

Researchers discovered that 3I/ATLAS contains an exceptionally high amount of this deuterium-rich water. While small amounts of heavy water exist on Earth and in comets within our solar system, the levels found in 3I/ATLAS were dramatically higher.

"The amount of deuterium with respect to ordinary hydrogen in water is higher than anything we've seen before in other planetary systems and planetary comets," Salazar Manzano said.

According to the researchers, the deuterium ratio in the comet was about 30 times higher than what has been measured in comets from our solar system and roughly 40 times higher than the ratio found in Earth's oceans.

Clues About a Frozen Birthplace

Scientists use deuterium levels as a kind of chemical fingerprint that reveals the conditions present when celestial objects formed. By comparing these ratios with those found closer to home, researchers can infer what kind of environment produced the comet.

The team concluded that 3I/ATLAS likely formed in a much colder region with lower radiation levels than the environment that created the planets and comets in our solar system.

"This is proof that whatever the conditions were that led to the creation of our solar system are not ubiquitous throughout space," said Teresa Paneque-Carreño, co-leader of the study and assistant professor of astronomy at U-M. "That may sound obvious, but it's one of those things that you need to prove."

How Scientists Studied 3I/ATLAS

The researchers said the study was only possible because astronomers detected 3I/ATLAS early enough for detailed follow-up observations.

After the discovery, Salazar Manzano and collaborators secured observing time at the MDM Observatory in Arizona, where they detected some of the first signs of gas emissions from the comet (MDM stands for Michigan, Dartmouth and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, the observatory's original partners).

Salazar Manzano then teamed up with Paneque-Carreño, who brought expertise using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array, or ALMA, in Chile. ALMA's instruments are sensitive enough to distinguish deuterated water from ordinary water, allowing the team to precisely measure the ratio between the two.

The researchers say this marks the first time scientists have successfully performed this type of water analysis on an interstellar object.

"Being at the University of Michigan and having access to these facilities was the key to making this work possible," Salazar Manzano said. "We were part of a team that was very talented and very experienced in multiple areas, all of us complemented each other and that's what allowed us to analyze and interpret these data sets."

More Interstellar Visitors Could Be Found

The study also demonstrates that astronomers may soon be able to chemically analyze additional interstellar objects to better understand how planetary systems form across the galaxy.

So far, scientists have identified only three known interstellar objects entering our solar system, but researchers expect that number to rise as more advanced observatories begin searching the skies.

Paneque-Carreño emphasized that preserving dark night skies will be essential for spotting these faint visitors.

"We need to be taking care of our night skies and keeping them clear and dark so we can detect these tiny and faint objects," she said.

Additional support for the research came from the Michigan Society of Fellows and the Heising-Simons Foundation. ALMA is operated through a partnership involving the European Southern Observatory, the NSF and Japan's National Institutes of Nature Sciences in cooperation with the Republic of Chile.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about an interstellar comet with an unusual water composition, providing new insights into planetary system formation beyond our solar system. This has broad implications for astronomy and our understanding of the galaxy, representing a meaningful breakthrough in space science with detailed context and analysis.
articles.personality_title: Alien comet 3I/ATLAS contains unusual heavy water unlike anything in our solar system
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Less than a year ago, astronomers spotted a comet called 3I/ATLAS passing through our solar system. It is only the third known comet from outside our solar system, offering a rare chance to learn about distant parts of our galaxy.

**What happened** – Scientists at the University of Michigan studied the comet’s water and found it contains a very high amount of heavy water. Heavy water has hydrogen atoms with an extra neutron, called deuterium. The amount of this heavy water in 3I/ATLAS is about 30 to 40 times higher than what we see in comets or oceans on Earth.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that 3I/ATLAS formed in a much colder and less radioactive place than our solar system. The unusual water acts like a chemical fingerprint, telling us that not all planetary systems form under the same conditions. This is the first time scientists have measured such water on an interstellar object.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to find and study more comets from outside our solar system. Using powerful telescopes like ALMA in Chile, they can learn how planets form in other parts of the galaxy. Protecting dark skies is important because it helps astronomers spot these faint visitors.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The comet 3I/ATLAS contains strange heavy water that reveals it was born in a much colder place than our solar system, opening new paths to study planetary formation beyond Earth’s neighborhood.
articles.personality_title_fr: La comète extraterrestre 3I/ATLAS contient une eau lourde inhabituelle jamais vue dans notre système solaire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a moins d’un an, des astronomes ont repéré une comète appelée 3I/ATLAS traversant notre système solaire. C’est seulement la troisième comète connue venue de l’extérieur de notre système solaire, offrant une occasion rare d’en apprendre davantage sur des régions lointaines de notre galaxie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université du Michigan ont étudié l’eau de la comète et ont découvert qu’elle contient une quantité très élevée d’eau lourde. L’eau lourde contient des atomes d’hydrogène avec un neutron supplémentaire, appelé deutérium. La quantité d’eau lourde dans 3I/ATLAS est environ 30 à 40 fois plus élevée que celle présente dans les comètes ou les océans terrestres.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que 3I/ATLAS s’est formée dans un endroit beaucoup plus froid et moins irradié que notre système solaire. L’eau inhabituelle agit comme une empreinte chimique, indiquant que tous les systèmes planétaires ne se forment pas dans les mêmes conditions. C’est la première fois que des scientifiques mesurent ce type d’eau sur un objet interstellaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent trouver et étudier davantage de comètes venues de l’extérieur de notre système solaire. Avec des télescopes puissants comme ALMA au Chili, ils pourront mieux comprendre comment les planètes se forment dans d’autres parties de la galaxie. Protéger les ciels sombres est important pour aider les astronomes à repérer ces visiteurs faibles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La comète 3I/ATLAS contient une eau lourde étrange qui révèle qu’elle est née dans un endroit beaucoup plus froid que notre système solaire, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour étudier la formation des planètes au-delà de notre voisinage terrestre.
articles.personality_title_es: La cometa interestelar 3I/ATLAS contiene agua pesada inusual nunca vista en nuestro sistema solar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace menos de un año, los astrónomos detectaron una cometa llamada 3I/ATLAS que pasó por nuestro sistema solar. Es solo la tercera cometa confirmada que viene de fuera de nuestro sistema solar, ofreciendo una oportunidad rara para aprender sobre partes lejanas de nuestra galaxia.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Michigan estudiaron el agua de la cometa y encontraron que contiene una gran cantidad de agua pesada. El agua pesada tiene átomos de hidrógeno con un neutrón extra, llamado deuterio. La cantidad de esta agua pesada en 3I/ATLAS es aproximadamente 30 a 40 veces mayor que la que se ve en cometas o en los océanos de la Tierra.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que 3I/ATLAS se formó en un lugar mucho más frío y con menos radiación que nuestro sistema solar. El agua inusual actúa como una huella química que indica que no todos los sistemas planetarios se forman bajo las mismas condiciones. Es la primera vez que los científicos miden este tipo de agua en un objeto interestelar.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan encontrar y estudiar más cometas de fuera de nuestro sistema solar. Usando telescopios potentes como ALMA en Chile, pueden aprender cómo se forman los planetas en otras partes de la galaxia. Proteger los cielos oscuros es importante para ayudar a los astrónomos a detectar estos visitantes tenues.

**Resumen en una frase** – La cometa 3I/ATLAS contiene agua pesada extraña que revela que nació en un lugar mucho más frío que nuestro sistema solar, abriendo nuevas formas de estudiar la formación planetaria más allá de nuestro vecindario terrestre.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing comet streaking gently through a starry cosmic landscape, its icy nucleus shimmering with intricate crystalline patterns symbolizing heavy deuterium-rich water, passing near stylized observatory domes and radio telescopes under a clear, dark sky, evoking the quiet, careful study and discovery of this unique interstellar visitor.

articles.title: The Universe’s biggest black holes may be forged in violent mergers
articles.summary: The Universe’s biggest black holes may not be born giants after all. Scientists analyzing gravitational-wave signals from dozens of black hole collisions found evidence that the heaviest black holes are likely “cosmic recyclers” — formed through repeated smashups inside incredibly crowded star clusters. These violent chain reactions appear to create a distinct class of rapidly spinning black holes that stand apart from ordinary ones formed by dying stars.
articles.content:
Scientists studying gravitational waves believe they may have uncovered how the Universe creates its biggest black holes. Instead of forming directly from collapsing stars, these enormous objects appear to grow through repeated black hole collisions inside extremely crowded star clusters.

The new research, led by Cardiff University, examined version 4.0 of LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA's Gravitational-Wave Transient Catalog (GWTC4), which contains 153 reliable detections of merging black holes.

Researchers focused on whether the largest black holes in the catalog could be "second-generation" objects. In this scenario, black holes formed from dying stars collide with each other, then merge again in dense stellar environments where stars are packed up to a million times more tightly than around our Sun.

The findings, published in Nature Astronomy, suggest the most massive black holes detected through gravitational waves belong to a separate class with a very different history from smaller black holes.

Gravitational Waves Reveal Two Black Hole Populations

"Gravitational-wave astronomy is now doing more than counting black hole mergers," explains lead author Dr. Fabio Antonini from Cardiff University's School of Physics and Astronomy.

"It is starting to reveal how black holes grow, where they grow, and what that tells us about the lives and deaths of massive stars. This is exciting because we can use the information to test our understanding of how stars and clusters evolve in the Universe."

By analyzing the gravitational-wave signals, the team identified two distinct groups:

a lower-mass population consistent with ordinary stellar collapse

a higher-mass population whose spins appear exactly like those expected from hierarchical mergers in dense star clusters

Researchers say the spin behavior of the heavier black holes was especially revealing.

"What surprised us most was how clearly the high-mass black holes stand out as a separate population," recalls co-author Dr. Isobel Romero-Shaw, Ernest Rutherford Fellow at Cardiff University.

"Unlike the lower-mass systems we analyzed, which were generally slowly-spinning, the higher-mass systems are consistent with having more rapid spins, oriented in seemingly random directions. This is the exact signature you would expect if black holes were repeatedly merging in dense star clusters.

"That makes the cluster origin much more compelling than it was with earlier catalogs."

Evidence for the Black Hole "Mass Gap"

The study also strengthens evidence for a mysterious "mass gap" predicted by astrophysicists for decades. According to this theory, stars above a certain size should explode so violently that they are destroyed completely instead of collapsing into black holes.

This would create a forbidden range where black holes formed directly from stars should not exist.

The researchers identified this transition in black holes with masses around 45 times greater than the Sun.

Dr. Antonini said: "In our study we find evidence for the long-predicted pair-instability mass gap -- a range of masses where stars are not expected to leave behind black holes at all. Gravitational-wave detectors have successfully found black holes that appear to sit in or near that gap, which we identify at around 45 solar masses.

"So, the key question now is are these black holes telling us that our models of stellar evolution are wrong, or are they being made in another way?

"The biggest black holes in the current sample seem to be telling us about cluster dynamics, not just stellar evolution.

"Above about 45 solar masses the spin distribution changes in a way that is hard to explain with normal stellar binaries alone but is naturally explained if these black holes have already been through earlier mergers in dense clusters."

Black Holes Could Help Scientists Study Nuclear Physics

The researchers say the discoveries may eventually help scientists investigate processes deep inside massive stars.

The team used the transition near the mass gap to study an important nuclear reaction linked to helium burning in stellar cores.

"In the future, gravitational-wave data may help scientists study nuclear physics, because the mass limit set by pair instability depends on the nuclear reactions taking place in the cores of massive stars," added co-author Dr. Fani Dosopoulou, a research associate at Cardiff University.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding how the Universe's biggest black holes form, revealing new insights into black hole growth and stellar evolution through gravitational-wave astronomy. This discovery has broad implications for astrophysics and nuclear physics, benefiting the general scientific community and expanding human knowledge about the cosmos.
articles.personality_title: New study shows biggest black holes form from repeated cosmic collisions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered how the Universe’s largest black holes are created. Unlike smaller black holes formed when stars die, the origin of these massive black holes remained unclear.

**What happened** – Researchers from Cardiff University studied data from gravitational-wave observatories LIGO, Virgo, and KAGRA. They analyzed 153 black hole mergers and found evidence that the heaviest black holes form through multiple collisions inside very crowded star clusters, where stars and black holes are packed tightly together.

**Impact** – This discovery reveals two groups of black holes: smaller ones formed directly from dying stars, and larger ones created by repeated mergers. The larger black holes spin faster and in random directions, matching predictions for black holes that have collided multiple times. The study also supports the idea of a “mass gap,” where black holes of certain sizes shouldn’t form from stars alone, suggesting these big black holes grow differently.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will use gravitational-wave data to learn more about how stars and black holes evolve. This research might also help study nuclear reactions inside stars, improving our understanding of how stars live and die.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Universe’s biggest black holes likely grow through repeated collisions in crowded star clusters, not just from single dying stars.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude révèle que les plus gros trous noirs se forment par collisions cosmiques répétées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se sont longtemps demandé comment se forment les plus grands trous noirs de l’Univers. Contrairement aux plus petits trous noirs créés par la mort des étoiles, l’origine de ces objets gigantesques restait mystérieuse.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Cardiff ont étudié les données des observatoires d’ondes gravitationnelles LIGO, Virgo et KAGRA. Ils ont analysé 153 fusions de trous noirs et trouvé des preuves que les trous noirs les plus lourds se forment par plusieurs collisions dans des amas d’étoiles très denses, où étoiles et trous noirs sont très proches.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre qu’il existe deux groupes de trous noirs : les petits, issus directement d’étoiles mortes, et les gros, créés par des fusions répétées. Les gros trous noirs tournent plus vite et dans des directions aléatoires, ce qui correspond aux prédictions pour des trous noirs ayant fusionné plusieurs fois. L’étude confirme aussi l’existence d’un « vide de masse », une taille de trous noirs qui ne devrait pas exister si formés uniquement par des étoiles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont utiliser les données des ondes gravitationnelles pour mieux comprendre l’évolution des étoiles et des trous noirs. Cette recherche pourrait aussi aider à étudier les réactions nucléaires dans les étoiles, améliorant notre connaissance de leur vie et mort.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les plus gros trous noirs de l’Univers grandissent probablement grâce à des collisions répétées dans des amas d’étoiles denses, pas seulement à partir d’étoiles mourantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio muestra que los agujeros negros más grandes se forman por colisiones cósmicas repetidas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han querido saber durante mucho tiempo cómo se forman los agujeros negros más grandes del Universo. A diferencia de los más pequeños, que aparecen cuando mueren las estrellas, el origen de los gigantescos agujeros negros era desconocido.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Cardiff analizaron datos de los observatorios de ondas gravitacionales LIGO, Virgo y KAGRA. Estudiaron 153 fusiones de agujeros negros y encontraron evidencias de que los más pesados se forman por múltiples colisiones dentro de cúmulos estelares muy densos, donde las estrellas y agujeros negros están muy juntos.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que existen dos grupos de agujeros negros: los pequeños, creados directamente por estrellas moribundas, y los grandes, formados por fusiones repetidas. Los agujeros negros grandes giran más rápido y en direcciones aleatorias, lo que coincide con lo esperado si han chocado varias veces. El estudio también confirma la existencia de un “vacío de masa”, una zona donde no deberían existir agujeros negros formados solo por estrellas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán datos de ondas gravitacionales para entender mejor cómo evolucionan las estrellas y agujeros negros. Esta investigación también podría ayudar a estudiar reacciones nucleares dentro de las estrellas, mejorando nuestro conocimiento sobre su vida y muerte.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los agujeros negros más grandes del Universo probablemente crecen mediante colisiones repetidas en cúmulos estelares densos, no solo por la muerte de estrellas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a cosmic dance inside a dense star cluster, showing multiple glowing black spheres merging together with swirling, radiant trails of light symbolizing gravitational waves, set against a deep space backdrop of softly colored stars and nebulae in natural blues, purples, and golds.

articles.title: NASA, Industry Advance High Performance Spaceflight Computing
articles.summary: For decades, NASA has advanced on-board spacecraft computer processors that coordinate and execute the functions needed to support mission success. Space computing originated in the 1960s with the Apollo Guidance Computers, which were pivotal for guidance, navigation, and control computations during NASA’s first Moon missions. For decades, radiation-hardened processors have been the backbone of the […]
articles.content:
High Performance Spaceflight Computing System on Chip NASA/Ryan Lannom

For decades, NASA has advanced on-board spacecraft computer processors that coordinate and execute the functions needed to support mission success.

Space computing originated in the 1960s with the Apollo Guidance Computers, which were pivotal for guidance, navigation, and control computations during NASA’s first Moon missions. For decades, radiation-hardened processors have been the backbone of the agency’s space exploration missions.

NASA has landed computers on other planets and operated them for years in extreme conditions, as demonstrated by the Mars rovers. These computer processors have also powered several NASA orbiters, capsules, and space telescopes.

While legacy processors have enabled some of NASA’s greatest achievements, the next generation of space missions will increase in complexity and length, which will benefit from greater computing power, autonomy, and resilience. To meet the needs of this challenge, NASA and industry leader Microchip Technology Inc. entered a public, private partnership combining agency and commercial investments to develop a new solution: High-Performance Spaceflight Computing.

Advanced Computing

The High-Performance Spaceflight Computing project is a next-generation system-on-chip that delivers over 100 times the computing capability of current space processors. By integrating computing and networking into a single device, this technology significantly reduces system cost and power consumption. Its scalable architecture allows unused functions to power down, optimizing energy efficiency for critical operations.

The High-Performance Spaceflight Computing family of processors includes multiple distinct but compatible technologies for scalable mission needs. The radiation-hardened version of the processor is built for geosynchronous, deep-space, and long-duration missions to the Moon, Mars, and beyond, capable of operating in harsh environments while supporting real-time autonomous tasks. Tailored for the commercial space sector, the radiation-tolerant version of the processor provides fault tolerance and cybersecurity for low Earth orbit satellites.

High Performance Spaceflight Computing System on Chip NASA/Ryan Lannom

Using advanced Ethernet to connect multiple sensors or cluster several chips, High-Performance Spaceflight Computing technology allows spacecraft to process massive amounts of data onboard and autonomously make real-time decisions, such as driving rovers at high speeds or filtering scientific images. Continuous system health monitoring and an integrated security controller ensure these complex operations remain safe and reliable.

Computing power for Golden Age of Exploration

The High-Performance Spaceflight Computing technology is a nationwide, public-private development effort anchored by NASA, Microchip, and a broad ecosystem of academic and industry partners. This collaboration reinforces U.S. leadership in spaceflight computing, strengthens supply chain resilience and security, stimulates regional economies, and drives innovation and high-tech workforce development across the nation.

This new technology has the potential for use on all future space missions, but unlike traditional space-specific chips, High-Performance Spaceflight Computing has a design platform for other Earth-based uses.

Adopting the same high-performance computing, network switching, high-reliability and cybersecurity technologies, the company’s processors enable mission-critical edge computing for Earth-based industries such as automotive, aviation, consumer electronics, industrial systems, and aerospace. These potential applications include drones, energy grids, medical equipment, communication services, artificial intelligence, and data transmission.

By leveraging a common technology base across space and terrestrial markets, High-Performance Spaceflight Computing helps strengthen domestic industrial capabilities and reduce risk and cost for both government and commercial users.

The Space Technology Mission Directorate’s Game Changing Development program based at NASA’s Langley Research Center in Hampton, Virginia, and NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory led the end-to-end maturation of NASA’s High-Performance Spaceflight Computing by developing mission requirements, funding competitive industry studies, selecting and contracting with Microchip, and guiding the project through design reviews and the project life cycle to delivery.

To learn more about these chips, visit:

https://go.nasa.gov/4cIGUKu

By: Jessica Jelke
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant advancement in spaceflight computing technology developed through a public-private partnership led by NASA. This breakthrough offers over 100 times the computing power of current space processors, enabling more complex, autonomous, and resilient space missions to the Moon, Mars, and beyond. The technology also has broad potential applications on Earth, strengthening industrial capabilities and cybersecurity. The story has clear positive real-world impact, broad significance for future space exploration and terrestrial industries, and sufficient detail on the innovation and collaboration involved.
articles.personality_title: NASA and Industry Develop Powerful New Spaceflight Computer Chip
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**  
NASA has been creating computer processors for spacecraft since the 1960s, starting with the Apollo Guidance Computers that helped astronauts reach the Moon. These special processors are designed to work in space, where radiation and tough conditions can damage regular electronics.

**What happened**  
NASA partnered with Microchip Technology Inc. and other organizations to build a new type of space computer chip called High-Performance Spaceflight Computing (HPSC). This chip is more than 100 times faster than current space processors and combines computing and networking in one device. It can save energy by turning off parts not in use and is designed to work on missions to the Moon, Mars, and beyond. There are versions for deep space and for satellites closer to Earth.

**Impact**  
This new chip lets spacecraft process large amounts of data quickly and make decisions by themselves, such as driving rovers or managing scientific instruments. It also includes strong security and health monitoring to keep missions safe. The technology strengthens U.S. leadership in space technology and can also be used on Earth in areas like drones, medical equipment, and energy systems, helping industries work better and more securely.

**What's next step**  
NASA and its partners will continue testing and improving the chip to prepare it for future space missions. The technology may also be adapted for more Earth-based uses, supporting innovation in many industries and helping develop skilled workers in technology fields.

**One-sentence takeaway**  
NASA’s new High-Performance Spaceflight Computing chip offers much faster, smarter, and safer computing for future space missions and many Earth applications.
articles.personality_title_fr: NASA et l’industrie développent une nouvelle puce informatique puissante pour les vols spatiaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**  
Depuis les années 1960, la NASA crée des processeurs informatiques pour les engins spatiaux, à commencer par les ordinateurs de guidage Apollo qui ont permis aux astronautes d’atteindre la Lune. Ces processeurs spéciaux sont conçus pour fonctionner dans l’espace, où les radiations et les conditions difficiles peuvent endommager l’électronique classique.

**Ce qui s’est passé**  
La NASA a collaboré avec Microchip Technology Inc. et d’autres partenaires pour développer un nouveau type de puce informatique spatiale appelée High-Performance Spaceflight Computing (HPSC). Cette puce est plus de 100 fois plus rapide que les processeurs actuels et combine calcul et réseau dans un seul appareil. Elle peut économiser de l’énergie en désactivant les parties inutilisées et est conçue pour les missions vers la Lune, Mars et au-delà. Il existe des versions pour l’espace lointain et pour les satellites en orbite basse.

**Impact**  
Cette nouvelle puce permet aux engins spatiaux de traiter rapidement de grandes quantités de données et de prendre des décisions de manière autonome, comme conduire des rovers ou gérer des instruments scientifiques. Elle comprend aussi une sécurité renforcée et une surveillance permanente pour garantir la sûreté des missions. Cette technologie renforce le leadership américain en technologie spatiale et peut aussi être utilisée sur Terre dans des domaines comme les drones, les équipements médicaux et les systèmes énergétiques, aidant ainsi les industries à être plus efficaces et sécurisées.

**Prochaines étapes**  
La NASA et ses partenaires continueront à tester et améliorer la puce pour la préparer aux futures missions spatiales. La technologie pourrait aussi être adaptée pour davantage d’applications terrestres, soutenant l’innovation dans de nombreuses industries et favorisant la formation de spécialistes technologiques.

**En une phrase**  
La nouvelle puce High-Performance Spaceflight Computing de la NASA offre un calcul beaucoup plus rapide, intelligent et sûr pour les missions spatiales futures et de nombreuses applications sur Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: NASA e industria desarrollan un nuevo chip informático potente para vuelos espaciales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**  
Desde los años 60, la NASA ha creado procesadores informáticos para naves espaciales, empezando con las computadoras de guía Apollo que ayudaron a los astronautas a llegar a la Luna. Estos procesadores especiales están diseñados para funcionar en el espacio, donde la radiación y las condiciones difíciles pueden dañar los equipos electrónicos comunes.

**Qué pasó**  
La NASA se asoció con Microchip Technology Inc. y otros socios para construir un nuevo tipo de chip informático espacial llamado High-Performance Spaceflight Computing (HPSC). Este chip es más de 100 veces más rápido que los procesadores espaciales actuales y combina computación y redes en un solo dispositivo. Puede ahorrar energía apagando las partes que no se usan y está diseñado para misiones a la Luna, Marte y más allá. Hay versiones para el espacio profundo y para satélites en órbita baja.

**Impacto**  
Este nuevo chip permite que las naves procesen grandes cantidades de datos rápidamente y tomen decisiones por sí mismas, como conducir rovers o manejar instrumentos científicos. También incluye seguridad fuerte y monitoreo continuo para mantener las misiones seguras. Esta tecnología fortalece el liderazgo de EE.UU. en tecnología espacial y también puede usarse en la Tierra en áreas como drones, equipos médicos y sistemas energéticos, ayudando a que las industrias funcionen mejor y con más seguridad.

**Próximos pasos**  
La NASA y sus socios seguirán probando y mejorando el chip para prepararlo para futuras misiones espaciales. La tecnología también podría adaptarse para más usos en la Tierra, apoyando la innovación en muchas industrias y ayudando a formar trabajadores especializados en tecnología.

**Una frase para recordar**  
El nuevo chip High-Performance Spaceflight Computing de la NASA ofrece un poder de cálculo mucho más rápido, inteligente y seguro para futuras misiones espaciales y muchas aplicaciones en la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a futuristic spacecraft interior featuring a glowing, intricately designed microchip at its core, connected by delicate, branching circuits resembling neural pathways that extend outward to multiple sensor nodes and small rover silhouettes visible through a softly lit window, symbolizing advanced spaceflight computing powering exploration beyond Earth.

articles.title: I Am Artemis: Anton Kiriwas
articles.summary: Listen to this audio excerpt from Anton Kiriwas, senior technical integration manager for NASA’s Exploration Ground Systems Program: When Anton Kiriwas first spotted an image of the Moon and Mars hanging over a job fair booth while in college, it captured his imagination, yet felt like a dream too distant to chase. He had no […]
articles.content:
Listen to this audio excerpt from Anton Kiriwas, senior technical integration manager for NASA’s Exploration Ground Systems Program:













0:00 / 0:00 Your browser does not support the audio element.

When Anton Kiriwas first spotted an image of the Moon and Mars hanging over a job fair booth while in college, it captured his imagination, yet felt like a dream too distant to chase. He had no way of knowing that years later he would play a critical role in NASA’s Artemis missions, helping launch humans back to the Moon for the first time in more than half a century.

Kiriwas’ journey to NASA began during the Space Shuttle Program, while he was working for United Launch Alliance, the same organization behind the memorable Moon and Mars booth that he passed by in college. Not long after, he joined NASA as a civil servant, designing electrical systems that set him on a path toward his current role with Exploration Ground Systems as senior technical integration manager. In simpler terms, Kiriwas is a problem solver.

My official title is way too long – what I do is pretty simple: I solve problems for the ground systems. Our goal is to process, launch, and recover the spacecraft. There are a lot of ground systems that are used to go do that and a lot of people involved. A big part of my job is to go solve all the problems that come. Anton Kiriwas Senior Technical Integration Manager, Exploration Ground Systems Program

A core part of Kiriwas’s role is to serve as a launch project engineer. Strategically positioned at the integration console in the center of Firing Room 1 of the Launch Control Center at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, he acts as a bridge for the test management and engineering teams. Kiriwas, along with the other launch project engineers, reports directly to the launch director, making the final technical recommendation on any issues that may arise during launch countdown. From this seat, he works across all engineering disciplines, united under one mission: launch the spacecraft and crew safely.

Anton Kiriwas, senior technical integration manager and senior launch project engineer with NASA’s Exploration Ground Systems Program participates in an Artemis II launch countdown simulation inside Firing Room 1 in the Launch Control Center at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida on Wednesday, Oct. 8, 2025. The simulations go through launch day scenarios to help launch team members test software and make adjustments if needed during countdown operations. NASA/Glenn Benson

Despite the intensity of launch day, Kiriwas describes it can often feel easier than the hundreds of rehearsals and simulations leading up to it. The team trains rigorously, preparing for every scenario imaginable. The ideal day is smooth and uneventful, but when it’s not, he and the team are ready.

I’m in my element when there is a problem. Anton Kiriwas Senior Technical Integration Manager, Exploration Ground Systems Program

When an issue arises, Kiriwas and his team begin asking the basic questions: ‘What are the requirements? Which systems are affected? Who needs to be involved?’ He pulls the technical community together to work through the situation, come up with any troubleshooting, and ultimately give the recommendation for a “go” or “no-go” for launch. It takes clarity, experience, and discipline, especially in moments when excitement is running high.

“There is adrenaline to get to launch, but you want to be careful to never let that turn into ‘launch fever,’” said Kiriwas. “We need to launch exactly when we’re ready and not a moment before.”

Anton Kiriwas, a launch project engineer for the Artemis I mission, monitors operations from his position in Firing Room 1 as Artemis teams conduct a launch simulation for the Artemis I launch inside the Rocco A. Petrone Launch Control Center at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida on Oct. 27, 2022. NASA/Ben Smegelsky

With Artemis II complete, Kiriwas continues applying his problem‑solving expertise, analyzing lessons learned, and shaping future mission requirements. Artemis III hardware is currently being processed at NASA Kennedy, and the teams are carefully preparing the next steps of NASA’s return to the lunar surface.

“There’s a million little pieces that go into this, and I get to be a part of it,” said Kiriwas.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the inspiring journey and critical role of Anton Kiriwas in NASA's Artemis program, which aims to return humans to the Moon. It focuses on a significant space exploration mission with broad societal interest and provides detailed context about his contributions and the program's impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA engineer Anton Kiriwas plays key role in Artemis Moon missions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Anton Kiriwas is a senior technical integration manager at NASA working on the Artemis program, which aims to send humans back to the Moon after more than 50 years. His journey started when he saw an inspiring image of the Moon and Mars at a job fair during college.

**What happened** – After working with United Launch Alliance, Kiriwas joined NASA and now helps manage the ground systems that prepare spacecraft for launch. He works in the Launch Control Center at Kennedy Space Center, solving problems during launch countdowns and making sure everything is ready for a safe takeoff.

**Impact** – Kiriwas’s work is vital because launching spacecraft involves many complex systems and people. His role as a problem solver helps keep the Artemis missions on track, ensuring that astronauts can return safely to the Moon. His story shows how dedication and teamwork make space exploration possible.

**What's next step** – With Artemis II completed, Kiriwas and his team are learning from the mission and preparing for Artemis III, which will bring humans back to the lunar surface. They are currently testing and processing equipment at Kennedy Space Center to get ready for upcoming launches.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Anton Kiriwas’s problem-solving skills are essential to NASA’s mission to safely return humans to the Moon through the Artemis program.
articles.personality_title_fr: Anton Kiriwas, ingénieur NASA, joue un rôle clé dans les missions lunaires Artemis
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Anton Kiriwas est un responsable principal de l’intégration technique à la NASA, travaillant sur le programme Artemis qui vise à renvoyer des humains sur la Lune après plus de 50 ans. Son parcours a commencé lorsqu’il a vu une image inspirante de la Lune et de Mars lors d’un salon de l’emploi à l’université.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Après avoir travaillé pour United Launch Alliance, Kiriwas a rejoint la NASA. Il aide maintenant à gérer les systèmes au sol qui préparent les engins spatiaux au lancement. Il travaille dans le centre de contrôle des lancements au Kennedy Space Center, résolvant les problèmes pendant le compte à rebours et s’assurant que tout est prêt pour un décollage en toute sécurité.

**Impact** – Le travail de Kiriwas est vital car le lancement des engins spatiaux implique de nombreux systèmes complexes et personnes. Son rôle de résolveur de problèmes aide à maintenir les missions Artemis sur la bonne voie, garantissant la sécurité des astronautes. Son histoire montre comment le dévouement et le travail d’équipe rendent possible l’exploration spatiale.

**Prochaine étape** – Avec Artemis II terminé, Kiriwas et son équipe tirent des leçons de la mission et se préparent pour Artemis III, qui ramènera des humains sur la surface lunaire. Ils testent et préparent actuellement le matériel au Kennedy Space Center pour les prochains lancements.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les compétences en résolution de problèmes d’Anton Kiriwas sont essentielles à la mission de la NASA pour ramener en toute sécurité des humains sur la Lune grâce au programme Artemis.
articles.personality_title_es: Anton Kiriwas, ingeniero de la NASA, desempeña un papel clave en las misiones lunares Artemis
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Anton Kiriwas es un gerente senior de integración técnica en la NASA que trabaja en el programa Artemis, que busca enviar humanos de regreso a la Luna después de más de 50 años. Su camino comenzó cuando vio una imagen inspiradora de la Luna y Marte en una feria de empleo durante la universidad.

**Qué pasó** – Después de trabajar en United Launch Alliance, Kiriwas se unió a la NASA y ahora ayuda a gestionar los sistemas terrestres que preparan las naves espaciales para el lanzamiento. Trabaja en el Centro de Control de Lanzamientos en el Centro Espacial Kennedy, resolviendo problemas durante la cuenta regresiva y asegurando que todo esté listo para un despegue seguro.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de Kiriwas es vital porque lanzar naves espaciales implica muchos sistemas y personas complejas. Su papel como solucionador de problemas ayuda a mantener las misiones Artemis en marcha, asegurando que los astronautas regresen a salvo a la Luna. Su historia muestra cómo la dedicación y el trabajo en equipo hacen posible la exploración espacial.

**Próximo paso** – Con Artemis II completado, Kiriwas y su equipo están aprendiendo de la misión y preparándose para Artemis III, que llevará a humanos de nuevo a la superficie lunar. Actualmente están probando y procesando equipos en el Centro Espacial Kennedy para los próximos lanzamientos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las habilidades para resolver problemas de Anton Kiriwas son esenciales para la misión de la NASA de regresar de forma segura a los humanos a la Luna a través del programa Artemis.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing launch control room console with softly illuminated buttons and screens arranged like a puzzle being solved, silhouetted abstract hands gently connecting glowing lines representing teamwork and problem-solving, framed by a serene backdrop of the Moon and Mars hanging low in a twilight sky outside a large window.
